feedback.text: Please also include a prohibition for video game publishers to be required to provide an end-of-life plan for video games they sell to customers, as is currently being proposed by the "Stop Destroying Videogames" European citizens' initiative, which at the time of writing has already gathered more than 1.4 million signatures from EU citizens. The initiative's organizers have put this better than I can, so I urge you to take a look at the citizen's initiative and their proposal. Still, if you're looking for my personal take on the issue, and why I think the Digital Fairness Act should include legislation on the issue: - The video game industry is already the biggest branch of entertainment in the world, far bigger than movies and music (you don't have to take my word for it, but this is not a disputed fact). Legislation and regulation has not kept up with the rapid growth of the industry, and with the emergence of new industry practices. - The current practice of video game publishers - where they make an ever growing number of video games unplayable whenever they choose - is severely detrimental not just for consumers, but to our society as a whole (the preservation of cultural artifacts on its own should be more than sufficient reason to disallow this practice) - Hundreds of video games have already been killed, that is, no one will be able to play them again. Legislation won't bring them back, but at least it will prevent this from happening to games in the future. - Video games are currently intentionally developed such that they do not have an end-of-life plan. This has fairly obvious advantages for publishers: if they kill an old game, customers will have to buy a new game if they want to play. As a software developer, I can confidently state that if publishers were required to have end-of-life plans for their video games, then their studios would be able to accomplish this without too much trouble (i.e., it's both possible and feasible). Don't let lobbyists tell you otherwise: ask independent experts from the industry. This is not a controversial opinion, but I expect it will be the main argument against this initiative. Thank you for your consideration!
